_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-verified against CDC MMWR 2016 RMSF guidance (Biggs 2016), Dahlgren 2016 RMSF mortality data, IDSA tick-borne disease guidelines, and Parola 2013 Clin Microbiol Rev on global rickettsioses.

h1Title: Spotted Fever: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Spotted fever group rickettsioses are tick-borne infections caused by obligate intracellular gram-negative bacteria of the genus Rickettsia, which invade vascular endothelium and produce a systemic small-vessel vasculitis. Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF, Rickettsia rickettsii) is the most lethal, killing 20-30% of untreated patients in the Americas; Mediterranean, African, and Queensland tick-bite fevers are milder regional variants. Classic presentation combines fever, severe headache, myalgia, and a centripetal maculopapular rash spreading from wrists and ankles inward, reaching palms and soles in 50-70% of RMSF cases. Empirical doxycycline started within five days of symptom onset reduces mortality below 5%; delay beyond day five sharply worsens outcomes.

keyStats.prevalence: RMSF reported incidence ~10 cases per million population in southeastern US (CDC 2010-2018); Mediterranean spotted fever endemic across the Mediterranean basin with seasonal peaks
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; children under 10 carry the highest RMSF case-fatality rate (Dahlgren 2016, MMWR)
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal distribution — children 5-9 and adults 60-69 most commonly diagnosed in US surveillance
keyStats.globalCases: Several thousand RMSF cases reported in the Americas annually; African tick-bite fever (R. africae) is the leading rickettsiosis in returning sub-Saharan travelers

definition: Spotted fever group rickettsioses (ICD-10: A77) are zoonotic bacterial infections caused by tick-transmitted species of the genus Rickettsia, including R. rickettsii (Rocky Mountain spotted fever, Western Hemisphere), R. conorii (Mediterranean spotted fever, Boutonneuse fever), R. africae (African tick-bite fever), R. australis (Queensland tick typhus), and R. parkeri (Tidewater spotted fever, southeastern United States). The organisms are obligate intracellular gram-negative bacteria that target vascular endothelial cells lining small arteries, capillaries, and venules. Bacterial replication inside endothelium provokes lymphohistiocytic vasculitis, increased vascular permeability, microhemorrhage, and the petechial-maculopapular eruption that gives the group its name. Principal tick vectors vary by region: Dermacentor variabilis and D. andersoni in North America, Amblyomma cajennense in Latin America, Rhipicephalus sanguineus for Mediterranean spotted fever, and Amblyomma hebraeum for R. africae. Most cases occur during peak tick activity in late spring and summer. Disease severity ranges from a mild self-limited illness (R. africae, R. parkeri) to fulminant multi-organ failure (R. rickettsii). The infection is managed primarily by infectious disease specialists, but recognition and empirical treatment in emergency departments and primary care are critical because untreated RMSF can be fatal within 8-15 days of symptom onset.

typesClassification.description: The most severe form, found throughout the Americas. Vectors include Dermacentor variabilis (eastern US), D. andersoni (Rocky Mountain region), Rhipicephalus sanguineus (Arizona, Mexico), and Amblyomma cajennense (Central and South America). Mortality 20-30% untreated, 5-10% treated.

typesClassification.type: Mediterranean spotted fever (R. conorii, Boutonneuse fever)
typesClassification.description: Endemic around the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, Black Sea, and India. Transmitted by the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus. Hallmark inoculation eschar (tache noire) at the bite site occurs in 70% of cases. Mortality 1-3% with treatment.

Most common rickettsiosis in returning travelers from sub-Saharan Africa. Transmitted by Amblyomma hebraeum and A. variegatum cattle ticks. Often presents with multiple eschars and regional lymphadenopathy; mortality essentially nil.
Confined to eastern Australia. Vectored by Ixodes holocyclus. Eschar usually present; rash maculopapular or vesicular; mortality very low with prompt doxycycline.
Recognized in the southeastern United States since 2004. Transmitted by Amblyomma maculatum (Gulf Coast tick). Always presents with eschar, less severe than RMSF, no documented deaths to date.

primarySymptoms: Abrupt onset of fever above 38.5°C, often reaching 39-40°C within the first 48 hours, typically 2-14 days after a tick bite.
Severe frontal or generalized headache that is one of the most consistent early features and frequently more intense than the fever alone would predict.
Diffuse myalgia and arthralgia, especially involving the calves and lower back, often described as deep aching that worsens with movement.

Maculopapular rash appearing 2-5 days after fever onset on the wrists and ankles, then spreading centripetally to the trunk and reaching the palms and soles in 50-70% of Rocky Mountain spotted fever cases.
Progression of the rash from blanching macules to non-blanching petechiae and purpura in severe disease, reflecting underlying vasculitis and microhemorrhage.

Inoculation eschar (tache noire) — a 5-10 mm necrotic black-crusted lesion at the tick bite site — present in roughly 70% of Mediterranean and African spotted fevers but uncommon in Rocky Mountain spotted fever.
Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain occurring in over half of RMSF cases and frequently mimicking gastroenteritis or appendicitis in the first 72 hours.
Marked malaise and prostration that progresses rapidly over 3-5 days, often more severe than the patient's vital signs initially suggest.
Conjunctival injection and periorbital edema, seen in 30-40% of Mediterranean and African spotted fever cases.
Confusion, lethargy, or focal neurological signs in severe RMSF, indicating central nervous system vasculitis and a poor prognostic indicator if treatment has not yet begun.
earlyWarningSigns: Recent (within 14 days) outdoor exposure in an endemic region with potential tick contact, even when no bite was noticed
Fever with severe headache out of proportion to other symptoms before any rash appears
Mild thrombocytopenia, hyponatremia (sodium below 135 mmol/L), or elevated transaminases on initial blood work in a febrile patient with tick exposure
A small painless black-crusted ulcer (eschar) on the scalp, groin, axilla, or behind the knee in a returning traveler
Diffuse calf or back myalgia that worsens daily during the first three days of fever
emergencySigns: Petechial or purpuric rash with fever — suggests advanced vasculitis and impending shock; empirical doxycycline must start immediately while ruling out meningococcemia
Altered mental status, seizures, or focal neurological deficits in a febrile patient with possible tick exposure
Respiratory distress, hypoxia, or radiographic infiltrates suggesting acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)
Oliguria, rapidly rising creatinine, or new-onset acute kidney injury during a febrile illness
Distal gangrene, dusky digits, or skin necrosis indicating severe vasculitic ischemia

causes.cause: Rickettsia rickettsii infection via Dermacentor or Amblyomma tick bite
causes.description: Causes Rocky Mountain spotted fever throughout the Americas. The bacterium is transmitted in tick saliva typically after 6-10 hours of attachment, invades endothelial cells, and replicates within them to produce small-vessel vasculitis.

causes.cause: Rickettsia conorii infection via Rhipicephalus sanguineus (brown dog tick)
causes.description: Causes Mediterranean spotted fever (Boutonneuse fever) across southern Europe, North Africa, the Middle East, and parts of India. Domestic dogs serve as reservoirs and tick hosts, especially in warm urban environments.

causes.cause: Rickettsia africae infection via Amblyomma cattle ticks
causes.description: Causes African tick-bite fever in sub-Saharan Africa, the eastern Caribbean, and increasingly in returning travelers. Amblyomma ticks aggressively hunt hosts, so cluster outbreaks among safari groups and hikers are common.

causes.cause: Rickettsia parkeri and other emerging spotted fever rickettsiae
causes.description: Includes R. parkeri (Gulf Coast tick, southeastern US), R. australis (Queensland), R. honei (Flinders Island), R. sibirica, and R. slovaca. These cause regional spotted fever syndromes that share the eschar-fever-rash triad but with lower mortality than RMSF.

causes.cause: Outdoor exposure during peak tick season
causes.description: Hiking, camping, gardening, hunting, and military training in wooded or grassy habitat from April through September account for the majority of cases. Children playing outdoors and adults with occupational exposure are highest-risk.

causes.cause: Close contact with domestic dogs in endemic regions
causes.description: The brown dog tick (R. sanguineus) lives on dogs and in kennels, bringing R. conorii (Mediterranean) and increasingly R. rickettsii (notably in Arizona) into peri-domestic settings where children are exposed.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in an endemic region during peak tick season
riskFactors.description: RMSF concentrates in five US states — North Carolina, Tennessee, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Missouri — which together report over 60% of national cases (CDC surveillance 2010-2018). Mediterranean spotted fever peaks July-September.

Forestry workers, military personnel, agricultural workers, hunters, and hikers spend prolonged time in tick habitat. Use of repellents and permethrin-treated clothing substantially reduces exposure risk.
Children under 10 and adults over 70 have the highest RMSF case-fatality rates (Dahlgren 2016). Pediatric mortality reaches 4% even with treatment and remains far higher with delayed diagnosis.
G6PD deficiency is associated with fulminant RMSF and earlier death in case series. The reason is incompletely understood but may relate to oxidative stress in endothelial cells.

riskFactors.factor: Close exposure to dogs in peri-domestic settings
riskFactors.description: Brown dog tick (Rhipicephalus sanguineus) infestation of homes and yards drives the Arizona RMSF outbreaks among Native American communities and Mediterranean spotted fever in southern Europe.

riskFactors.factor: Delayed presentation or delayed doxycycline initiation
riskFactors.description: Mortality rises sharply when doxycycline is started after day 5 of illness. In the CDC RMSF case-fatality analysis, treatment delay was the single strongest predictor of death (Biggs 2016).

riskFactors.factor: Travel to sub-Saharan Africa with tick exposure
riskFactors.description: African tick-bite fever (R. africae) is now the second most common cause of fever in returning safari travelers after malaria; group exposures are typical.

affectedDemographics: US RMSF incidence highest in Native American communities in Arizona, where peri-domestic Rhipicephalus sanguineus outbreaks have produced case-fatality rates above 10% (CDC 2013)
Mediterranean spotted fever predominantly affects adults in southern Europe and North Africa during summer months
African tick-bite fever disproportionately affects international travelers, with attack rates of 4-5% reported in safari tourist cohorts
Children under 10 represent roughly one-third of US RMSF deaths despite being only a minority of cases, reflecting diagnostic delay and rapid disease progression
Rural and peri-urban populations carry higher exposure risk than dense urban populations across all spotted fever group infections
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of spotted fever group rickettsiosis rests on clinical suspicion in any febrile patient with potential tick exposure during the appropriate season. Laboratory confirmation lags behind the disease, so empirical treatment with doxycycline must begin before results return — CDC and IDSA both endorse this approach because the major preventable cause of RMSF mortality is treatment delay (Biggs 2016, MMWR). The reference confirmatory test is the indirect immunofluorescence assay (IFA) on paired acute and convalescent sera collected 2-4 weeks apart, with a four-fold rise in IgG titer establishing seroconversion. A single early titer is usually negative because antibodies do not appear until the second week of illness, so a negative test in the first 5 days does not exclude disease. Real-time PCR on whole blood, eschar swabs, or skin biopsy from a rash lesion has 70-90% sensitivity when performed early in untreated patients and produces same-day results in reference labs. Immunohistochemistry on biopsy of a rash lesion or eschar remains the gold standard for tissue confirmation. Routine laboratory findings supporting the diagnosis include thrombocytopenia (50-70% of cases), hyponatremia (50-60%), mild to moderate transaminitis, and a normal or slightly low white cell count with left shift. Key differentials are meningococcemia, measles, dengue, leptospirosis, secondary syphilis, drug eruptions, and viral hemorrhagic fevers — most can be excluded clinically or by parallel rapid testing while empirical doxycycline continues. Delaying treatment to await confirmation has repeatedly been shown to worsen survival.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference test for confirmation. A four-fold rise in IgG titer between acute and convalescent samples (2-4 weeks apart) confirms infection. Single early titers are insensitive and should not delay treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two standard blood draws separated by 2-4 weeks. Results from the second sample typically return within 7-10 days of submission to a reference lab.

diagnosticTests.test: Real-time PCR on whole blood, skin biopsy, or eschar swab
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects rickettsial DNA in the first week of illness. Sensitivity is 70-90% for skin biopsy and eschar swab in untreated patients, lower for whole blood.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small skin biopsy under local anesthetic, or a swab of an eschar after gentle crust removal. Reference labs return results within 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard tissue confirmation — demonstrates rickettsial antigens in endothelium of dermal vessels. Useful in fatal cases for post-mortem confirmation and in atypical presentations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy of a rash lesion or eschar under local anesthetic. Tissue is fixed in formalin and processed at a reference lab; results take 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports the diagnosis by showing the characteristic pattern: thrombocytopenia, hyponatremia, normal or low white cell count, mildly elevated creatinine.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return same day in most clinical settings and guide both diagnosis and supportive care decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mild transaminitis (AST and ALT elevated 2-4 times the upper limit of normal) is present in over half of cases and supports the diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, typically bundled with the metabolic panel. Same-day results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed when meningitis or encephalitis is suspected. Cerebrospinal fluid typically shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis and modest protein elevation; helps exclude bacterial meningitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at the bedside with local anesthetic; lasts 15-30 minutes. Results for cell count, glucose, protein, and Gram stain return within hours.

treatmentOverview: Doxycycline is the first-line treatment for every spotted fever group rickettsiosis in patients of all ages, including children and pregnant patients in the second and third trimesters when no acceptable alternative exists. CDC reaffirmed in 2017 that short courses of doxycycline do not produce clinically meaningful dental staining or enamel hypoplasia in children under 8, formally retiring earlier age-based contraindications (Todd 2015, Biggs 2016 MMWR). Adult dosing is 100 mg orally or intravenously twice daily; pediatric dosing is 2.2 mg per kilogram up to a 100 mg maximum twice daily. Treatment continues for at least 5-7 days and for a minimum of 3 days after the patient becomes afebrile, which typically occurs within 24-72 hours of starting therapy. Empirical doxycycline must be started on clinical suspicion alone in any febrile patient with possible tick exposure during the appropriate season — the single strongest predictor of death from Rocky Mountain spotted fever is treatment delay beyond day 5 of illness. Beta-lactam antibiotics, macrolides, sulfonamides, and fluoroquinolones are not effective; sulfa drugs in particular worsen rickettsial illness. For patients with true doxycycline anaphylaxis or in pregnancy where doxycycline cannot be used, chloramphenicol 50-75 mg per kilogram per day in four divided doses is the established alternative, although it is more difficult to obtain in many countries and carries its own toxicity profile. Severely ill patients require intravenous doxycycline, intensive care monitoring, and supportive measures: cautious fluid resuscitation balancing hypovolemia against the capillary-leak vasculitis, vasopressors for shock, mechanical ventilation for ARDS, and renal replacement therapy when needed. Anticoagulants and high-dose corticosteroids do not improve outcomes and are not routinely used. Post-treatment follow-up at 2-4 weeks documents clinical recovery and obtains the convalescent serology sample. Recovery from uncomplicated disease is rapid, but neurological and digital ischemic sequelae from severe RMSF can persist for months.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (adults 100 mg twice daily PO or IV; children 2.2 mg/kg twice daily to a 100 mg maximum)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy for every spotted fever group rickettsiosis at any age. Continue for 5-7 days and for at least 3 days after defervescence. CDC 2017 reaffirmed safety in children under 8 for short courses; outdated tooth-staining warnings no longer apply.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Defervescence within 24-72 hours in over 90% of patients when started by day 5 of illness; reduces RMSF mortality from 20-30% untreated to under 5% when started within the first five days (Dahlgren 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Chloramphenicol (50-75 mg/kg/day IV in four divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative when doxycycline is genuinely contraindicated — most notably in pregnancy near term where doxycycline must be avoided. Requires monitoring for dose-dependent marrow suppression and idiosyncratic aplastic anemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective but consistently inferior to doxycycline in observational comparisons; case-fatality is roughly twofold higher in chloramphenicol-treated RMSF (Holman 2001, Pediatrics).

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluids and vasopressor support
medicalTreatments.description: Cautious crystalloid resuscitation targeting perfusion while avoiding pulmonary edema in the setting of vasculitic capillary leak. Norepinephrine is the preferred vasopressor for distributive shock.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality in severe sepsis-equivalent presentations when combined with timely doxycycline; benefit not quantified separately for rickettsial shock.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical ventilation and lung-protective strategies
medicalTreatments.description: Used for the 5-10% of severe RMSF cases that progress to acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg predicted body weight) and prone positioning follow general ARDS protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces ARDS-attributable mortality by 20-30% when used per ARDSNet protocols; rickettsiae-specific data limited to case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for severe acute kidney injury from rickettsial vasculitis when fluid balance, electrolytes, or uremic complications cannot be managed medically.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recovery of renal function in the majority of survivors when rickettsial infection is controlled with doxycycline.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement or amputation of necrotic tissue
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for the 5% of severe RMSF survivors who develop irreversible gangrene of fingers, toes, or larger areas from vasculitic ischemia and microthrombosis. Decisions on level of amputation typically wait until demarcation is clear.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional outcomes depend on extent; early aggressive medical management is the primary determinant of avoiding surgery.

whySeeSpecialist: Infectious disease consultation is recommended for any patient hospitalized with suspected spotted fever group rickettsiosis, for diagnostic uncertainty between rickettsial disease and similar syndromes (meningococcemia, dengue, leptospirosis), for severe or atypical presentations, for pregnant patients where doxycycline use must be carefully weighed, and for returning international travelers with fever and eschar. Infectious disease specialists also coordinate confirmatory testing through reference laboratories.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician with experience in tropical and travel medicine if the exposure was overseas, or in tick-borne disease if exposure was domestic. Hospitals with rapid PCR access and reference-lab connections for rickettsial serology shorten the diagnostic loop. In rural endemic regions, family physicians and emergency clinicians who recognize the illness and start doxycycline empirically often save more lives than specialty consultation alone.
preventionStrategies: Wear long sleeves, long trousers, and closed shoes when entering tick habitat, tucking trousers into socks to create a physical barrier against questing ticks
Apply EPA-registered insect repellents containing 20-30% DEET to exposed skin and treat outer clothing and gear with 0.5% permethrin, which kills ticks on contact
Perform full-body tick checks within 2 hours of returning from tick habitat, paying particular attention to the scalp, behind the ears, axillae, groin, and behind the knees
Remove attached ticks promptly using fine-tipped tweezers grasping the tick as close to the skin as possible and pulling steadily upward without twisting
Treat pet dogs with veterinary tick preventives in regions where brown dog tick transmission occurs, especially Arizona and Mediterranean Europe
Reduce peri-domestic tick habitat by clearing leaf litter, mowing tall grass at yard edges, and applying acaricides to high-risk perimeter zones in endemic regions
lifestyleModifications: Schedule outdoor activities to avoid peak tick activity in late spring through early autumn when feasible, or apply enhanced repellent measures during those months
Shower within 2 hours of outdoor exposure to wash off unattached ticks and facilitate tick checks
Tumble-dry outdoor clothing on high heat for 10 minutes after outdoor exposure to kill any ticks on fabric
Educate household members and travel companions to recognize an eschar and the centripetal rash pattern, particularly when traveling to endemic regions
Keep a documented record of tick bites and outdoor exposure for the past 14 days when seeking care for unexplained fever

recommended: Adequate oral hydration during convalescence to support renal recovery, particularly after hyponatremia and acute kidney injury
Protein-rich diet during recovery to support repair of damaged endothelium and recover muscle mass lost during illness
Iron-rich foods or supplementation if anemia develops from microhemorrhage and disseminated intravascular coagulation
Light, easily digestible meals during the acute febrile phase when nausea and vomiting are prominent
avoid: Alcohol during and immediately after treatment, both for hepatic recovery and because alcohol can interact with hepatic drug metabolism
Dairy products and antacids within 2 hours of an oral doxycycline dose, as divalent cations significantly reduce drug absorption
Iron supplements within 2 hours of doxycycline for the same chelation reason — separate doses by at least 2-3 hours
Sun exposure without sunscreen during doxycycline therapy because the drug causes phototoxic skin reactions in 10-15% of patients

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest is appropriate during the acute febrile illness. Once afebrile and clinically improving, gradual return to normal activity over 1-3 weeks is typical. Severe RMSF survivors with residual neurological or cardiopulmonary impairment benefit from structured rehabilitation, including physical and occupational therapy as needed.
dailyManagement: Take doxycycline doses 12 hours apart with a full glass of water, sitting upright for at least 30 minutes to reduce pill esophagitis risk
Monitor temperature twice daily during the first week of treatment and report failure to defervesce within 72 hours to the treating clinician
Apply sunscreen and avoid prolonged sun exposure during the treatment course to prevent doxycycline phototoxicity
Track any new rash, bruising, bleeding, confusion, or decreased urine output and contact the clinician promptly
Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve in the first 48 hours — stopping early can permit relapse
prognosis: Outcome depends overwhelmingly on the time from symptom onset to first dose of doxycycline. Rocky Mountain spotted fever case fatality is 20-30% in untreated patients and 5-10% even with treatment; when doxycycline is started within the first five days, mortality falls below 5% in most modern series (Dahlgren 2016, MMWR). Mediterranean spotted fever has a treated mortality of 1-3%, with severe forms more common in older adults with underlying conditions. African tick-bite fever and Queensland tick typhus rarely cause death. Long-term sequelae after severe RMSF include cognitive impairment, peripheral neuropathy, sensorineural hearing loss, and persistent fatigue in roughly 10-15% of survivors. Around 5% of severe survivors require amputation of fingers, toes, or larger digits because of irreversible vasculitic ischemia. Children under 10 and adults over 70 have the worst outcomes, primarily because diagnosis is more often delayed in these groups.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 24-72 hours of starting doxycycline. Rash and constitutional symptoms fade over 7-14 days. Mild laboratory abnormalities (thrombocytopenia, transaminitis, hyponatremia) normalize over 1-2 weeks. Severe disease with multi-organ failure requires 2-6 weeks of hospital care, and full functional recovery from neurological or digital ischemic complications can extend 3-12 months.
complications: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) from pulmonary vasculitis and capillary leak — occurs in 5-10% of severe Rocky Mountain spotted fever cases
Acute kidney injury from rickettsial vasculitis, hypoperfusion, and rhabdomyolysis — reported in 20-30% of severe cases
Encephalitis or meningoencephalitis with seizures, confusion, and focal neurological deficits — a poor prognostic marker if present before doxycycline starts
Disseminated intravascular coagulation with petechial and purpuric rash, internal bleeding, and microvascular thrombosis
Peripheral gangrene of fingers, toes, ear lobes, or larger digits, occasionally requiring amputation in roughly 5% of severe survivors
Long-term cognitive impairment, peripheral neuropathy, and sensorineural hearing loss in 10-15% of severe survivors

supportResources.name: CDC — Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient and clinician information including diagnosis, treatment, and surveillance maps.

supportResources.name: CDC — Tick Removal and Bite Prevention
supportResources.description: Practical guidance on tick repellents, removal technique, and post-bite monitoring.

supportResources.name: WHO — Vector-Borne Diseases
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology and prevention guidance on tick-borne and other vector-borne infections.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lyme Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lyme disease produces an expanding erythema migrans rash centered on the tick bite (typically over 5 cm with central clearing), not a centripetal maculopapular rash on palms and soles. Onset is more gradual, fever is milder, and the causative agent is Borrelia burgdorferi rather than a Rickettsia species. Treatment is doxycycline for both, but Lyme rarely produces the severe vasculitis or thrombocytopenia of spotted fever.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue is mosquito-transmitted, presents with retro-orbital pain, severe myalgia, and a generalized maculopapular rash that often appears with defervescence rather than spreading centripetally. Thrombocytopenia is more profound in dengue, and there is no eschar. Travel history to dengue-endemic urban areas with mosquito exposure points to dengue rather than rickettsiosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is acquired through contact with water or soil contaminated by animal urine rather than ticks, and characteristically produces conjunctival suffusion, severe calf myalgia, and jaundice (Weil's disease). The rash, when present, is non-specific and palms and soles are typically spared. Confirmation is by serology or PCR for Leptospira species.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scarlet Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scarlet fever follows streptococcal pharyngitis and produces a diffuse sandpaper rash beginning on the trunk with circumoral pallor and a strawberry tongue. Patients are usually children with a sore throat history, there is no tick exposure or eschar, and palms and soles classically peel rather than carry the rash itself.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid fever is acquired through contaminated food or water, produces a stepwise rising fever over a week with relative bradycardia, abdominal pain, and sparse rose spots rather than a centripetal maculopapular rash. Cultures of blood and stool grow Salmonella Typhi rather than identifying a Rickettsia.

relatedConditions.name: Lyme Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Another tick-borne infection acquired in overlapping geographic regions; co-infection occasionally reported in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the differential for acute febrile illness with rash and multi-organ involvement, especially in tropical and subtropical regions.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential and frequent co-circulating pathogen in tropical endemic regions; thrombocytopenia overlaps with spotted fever findings.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Considered alongside rickettsioses in febrile returning travelers from South Asia and other endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Paratyphoid Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Enteric fever syndrome overlapping clinically with spotted fever in tropical settings; ruled out by blood and stool culture.

faqs.question: What is spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Spotted fever is a group of tick-borne Rickettsia infections including Rocky Mountain, Mediterranean, African, and Queensland forms. The bacteria infect blood vessel lining cells and cause fever, severe headache, muscle pain, and a spotted rash. RMSF can be fatal without prompt doxycycline.

faqs.question: How is spotted fever transmitted?
faqs.answer: Spotted fever spreads through bites from infected hard ticks. Vectors vary by Rickettsia species: Dermacentor and Amblyomma ticks in the Americas, Rhipicephalus sanguineus brown dog ticks in the Mediterranean, and Amblyomma cattle ticks in Africa. Most transmission needs 6-10 hours of attachment.

faqs.question: How long after a tick bite do symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms usually develop 2-14 days after an infected tick bite, with a median of 5-7 days. Many patients do not remember being bitten because the tick may have been small and painless. Any fever with severe headache within 2 weeks of potential tick exposure needs urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: What does the rash of spotted fever look like?
faqs.answer: The rash usually begins 2-5 days after fever onset as small pink macules on the wrists and ankles, then spreads inward to the trunk and reaches the palms and soles in 50-70% of RMSF cases. With progression it darkens to petechiae and purpura, reflecting damage to small blood vessels.

faqs.question: How is spotted fever diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis rests on clinical suspicion in any febrile patient with possible tick exposure. Confirmatory tests include paired immunofluorescence serology, PCR on blood or skin biopsy, and immunohistochemistry. Doxycycline starts empirically before results return because delay increases mortality.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Doxycycline is first-line at any age, including children and pregnant women when no acceptable alternative exists. Adult dose is 100 mg twice daily for at least 5-7 days and at least 3 days after fever resolves. Chloramphenicol is used only when doxycycline is genuinely contraindicated.

faqs.question: Is doxycycline safe for children with suspected spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. CDC reaffirmed in 2017 that short doxycycline courses for suspected rickettsial infection do not cause meaningful dental staining in children under 8. Untreated RMSF has a high case-fatality rate in children, so the benefit of doxycycline clearly outweighs the historical concern.

faqs.question: How deadly is Rocky Mountain spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Rocky Mountain spotted fever kills 20-30% of patients if untreated and 5-10% even with treatment. Mortality is highest when doxycycline is started after the fifth day of illness. Children under 10 and adults over 70 have the worst outcomes, largely because diagnosis is more often delayed.

faqs.question: Can spotted fever be prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention focuses on avoiding tick bites. Wear long sleeves in tick habitat, use EPA-registered repellents with DEET on skin and permethrin on clothing, perform full-body tick checks within 2 hours of exposure, and remove attached ticks promptly. There is no licensed spotted fever vaccine.

faqs.question: What is an eschar in spotted fever?
faqs.answer: An eschar is a 5-10 mm necrotic black-crusted lesion at the site of the infecting tick bite. It appears in roughly 70% of Mediterranean and African spotted fever cases but is uncommon in RMSF. Finding an eschar in a febrile traveler from a tick-endemic region strongly supports the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Where in the US is Rocky Mountain spotted fever most common?
faqs.answer: More than 60% of US cases come from five states: North Carolina, Tennessee, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Missouri. Arizona has ongoing outbreaks among Native American communities driven by peri-domestic brown dog ticks. Despite the name, the disease is uncommon in the Rocky Mountain region today.

faqs.question: How is African tick-bite fever different from RMSF?
faqs.answer: African tick-bite fever is caused by Rickettsia africae, transmitted by Amblyomma cattle ticks in sub-Saharan Africa, and usually presents with multiple eschars and regional lymphadenopathy. It is much milder than RMSF, rarely involves palms and soles, and essentially never causes death.

faqs.question: Can spotted fever recur?
faqs.answer: True recurrence after a fully treated infection is very rare. Most patients develop lasting immunity to the Rickettsia species that infected them. Reinfection with a different rickettsial species remains possible, especially in returning travelers or people with ongoing occupational exposure.

faqs.question: What blood tests are abnormal in spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Common findings include thrombocytopenia in 50-70% of cases, hyponatremia in 50-60%, mildly elevated liver enzymes, and a normal or slightly low white cell count. These changes support the diagnosis when they appear together with fever and tick exposure but are not specific on their own.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women take doxycycline for spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Doxycycline is generally avoided in pregnancy when alternatives exist. However, for severe suspected RMSF the benefit outweighs the small fetal risk because the alternative chloramphenicol is less effective. Decisions are individualized with infectious disease and obstetric input.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of severe RMSF?
faqs.answer: Severe RMSF can leave 10-15% of survivors with cognitive impairment, peripheral neuropathy, hearing loss, or persistent fatigue. Around 5% need amputation of fingers, toes, or larger digits from irreversible blood vessel damage. Early doxycycline prevents most of these complications.

faqs.question: How quickly does doxycycline work in spotted fever?
faqs.answer: Most patients defervesce within 24-72 hours of the first doxycycline dose. Failure to improve within 72 hours should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis or evaluation for complications. Even after fever resolves, antibiotics continue for at least 3 more days, typically a 5-7 day total.

faqs.question: Why are antibiotics other than doxycycline ineffective?
faqs.answer: Beta-lactams, macrolides, and fluoroquinolones do not reliably penetrate the intracellular compartment where Rickettsia replicates, and sulfonamides worsen rickettsial disease. Doxycycline distributes well into cells and has decades of supporting evidence, which is why it remains first-line.

faqs.question: Should everyone with a tick bite take antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Most tick bites do not transmit infection, and routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended for spotted fever rickettsioses. Watch for fever, headache, rash, or an eschar over the 14 days after the bite and seek care promptly if these develop. Doxycycline starts only when illness develops.

faqs.question: Can dogs spread spotted fever to humans?
faqs.answer: Dogs do not directly transmit Rickettsia, but they carry infected brown dog ticks (Rhipicephalus sanguineus) into homes and yards. This drives Mediterranean spotted fever in southern Europe and RMSF in parts of Arizona. Veterinary tick control on dogs is an important prevention step.
